script:
Hello, this is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. Thursday — here is your QQQ recap. The portfolio closed three point zero three percent down on the session. It was a broad and painful day across technology and consumer discretionary, driven by two names that did most of the damage.

Alphabet announced full-year capital expenditure guidance of roughly two hundred billion dollars. The market's response was immediate and unambiguous — the stock dropped more than seven percent in a single session, erasing tens of billions in market value before the close.

The Nasdaq fell more than two percent on Thursday, its steepest single-session decline in months, as weak big-tech earnings collided with a fresh macro threat: crude oil surging past one hundred dollars per barrel on reports that the Trump administration is warning of a significant strike on Iran. That combination — AI spending anxiety and reignited inflation fears — hit growth stocks from multiple directions simultaneously, pushing bond yields higher and amplifying the selling pressure across the portfolio.

Alphabet's session was the defining event of Thursday's trading. The company reported second-quarter adjusted earnings of nine dollars and eleven cents per share — dramatically ahead of Wall Street's consensus estimate of two dollars and eighty-eight cents, according to FactSet. The beat was substantial. But embedded in those results was a six dollar and twenty-six cent per share gain from equity securities fair value adjustments, which analysts were quick to strip out. More critically, the company raised its capital expenditure forecast again, projecting roughly two hundred billion dollars in spending for the full year. Investors interpreted that as a structural margin compression signal, not a growth catalyst. The stock finished down seven point one three percent. For this portfolio, Alphabet is the third-largest position at six point one eight percent of total holdings — that move alone was a meaningful drag on the session's result, as reported by multiple outlets including the Economic Times and finance.yahoo.com.

Now the full holdings rundown. Starting at the top: Nvidia was down one point five six percent on the session. The broader AI infrastructure trade came under pressure as Alphabet's capex announcement raised questions about return timelines across the sector. Watch for any follow-through selling in semiconductor names Friday. Apple was down one point three zero percent on the session. The Magnificent Seven group collectively erased seven hundred ninety-seven billion dollars in market value Thursday, its worst single day since the tariff-driven selloff in April of twenty twenty-five, per finance.yahoo.com. Apple, as a core member of that group, moved with the tape.

Alphabet was down seven point one three percent on the session — the full context was covered in the top story. This was the lead weight on the portfolio Thursday.

Turning to Micron Technology, which was up three point two zero percent on the session — the lone standout among primary semiconductor names. Micron bucked the sector-wide decline, suggesting some rotation into memory names as investors reassess AI infrastructure spending timelines. Watch for any analyst commentary on memory demand in the back half of twenty twenty-six.

Microsoft was down two point two four percent on the session. No company-specific news drove the move — this was broader sentiment weighing on cloud and enterprise software as AI capex concerns rippled across the sector.

Over at Amazon, the stock was down four point five seven percent on the session. The magnitude of that drop, well beyond the index decline, signals that investors are applying Alphabet's capex read-through directly to Amazon's own cloud and AI investment narrative. Watch Amazon's upcoming earnings closely for any updated spending guidance.

Advanced Micro Devices was down two point two nine percent on the session. The name tracked the broader semiconductor tape lower. No company-specific catalyst — this was sector correlation at work during a risk-off session.

Meanwhile, Tesla was down fourteen point five two percent on the session — the single worst performer in the portfolio by a wide margin. According to multiple sources including finance.yahoo.com and the Economic Times, Tesla's earnings results disappointed, with the selloff contributing directly to the broad Nasdaq decline. This was a significant position-level event given Tesla's three point zero nine percent portfolio weight.

Intel was down two point three three percent on the session. Notably, Intel reported second-quarter adjusted earnings of forty-two cents per share, nearly double the analyst estimate of twenty-two cents, per finance.yahoo.com. Despite the strong print, the stock could not hold gains against the broader market selloff.

Three things to watch in the next forty-eight hours. First, Amazon's upcoming earnings release — given Thursday's four point five seven percent decline and the AI capex read-through, guidance commentary on cloud spending will be critical for this portfolio. Second, Federal Reserve speakers — with crude oil above one hundred dollars and inflation fears back on the table, any Fed commentary on the rate path moves the entire growth stock complex. Third, oil prices — if crude sustains above one hundred dollars, expect continued pressure on Nasdaq valuations and bond yields into Friday's session.

This is The Portfolio Podcast for QQQ. We will be back same time the next business day with tomorrow's news. Watch whether the Alphabet and Tesla selloffs stabilize or whether the AI capex narrative continues to pressure the broader technology complex into the weekend.
